messages.content:
In groups of 5-6 students, you are required to design and implement a database to
address the requirements of a scenario provided by your tutor. Note that you will be
grouped with other students in the same seminar session.
As the scenario contains only a brief outline of a business case study, you will need to
state any business rules and assumptions that you have made in order to fill any gaps or
to address any ambiguities when putting together your database design. All the required
tables need to be implemented using Oracle APEX.
SUBMISSION A: Group Project Report
Each group member needs to submit a copy of their group’s report using the ‘ISDB
Group Project’ submission link set up on Blackboard. Any group member who fails to
submit a copy will be awarded zero marks. Any report that is submitted late will capped
at 40%. The report must use the assignment report template provided, containing the
following elements, in this order:
1. A header page which includes the name and ID of your team members and the
username and password of the APEX account(s) containing your group’s
implemented tables and reports/queries.
2. A list of any assumptions/clarifications you have made that have affected the
design of your database.
3. An ER diagram (using ‘Crow’s foot’ notation) providing the final design of your
database, which shows entities, attributes, primary and foreign keys, and named
relationships.
4. Entity specification forms documenting all your entities (using the template
provided).
5. The SQL scripts you used to create your tables (including all constraints) which
should match your entity specification forms.
6. A list of the SQL scripts (INSERT INTO ..commands) used to insert test data into
each of your tables, annotated to show that there is sufficient breadth and
volume of data to adequately test your queries/reports (see below).
2
7. 8 functional requirements generating reports/output that effectively
demonstrate the scope and functionality of the system, and for each: a) the SQL
script used to implement the requirement, and b) a screenshot of the run query
and output it produces. The queries you choose should critical queries illustrating
the breadth and potential of the implemented database, as well as your mastery
of SQL. Each script should be numbered and saved in your database.
8. A brief overview of the database features you would implement to ensure your
data is adequately secured.
9. A suggestion for one other database technology/information system that you
think might benefit the company, either now or in the future, providing a
rationale for this.
10. Evidence to show effective and timely project management using Scrum, the
contribution of each member to the project, namely attendance at team
meetings held in (and outside) your seminar sessions, sprint backlogs, tasks
uploaded to the group’s file Blackboard area and SQL scripts saved in members’
APEX account(s).

1. Header Page

Team Members:

1. Name: John Doe, ID: 12345
2. Name: Jane Smith, ID: 67890
3. Name: Alice Brown, ID: 24680
4. Name: Bob Johnson, ID: 13579
5. Name: Charlie Williams, ID: 98765

APEX Account(s) username/password:

Username: GroupABC
Password: DBProject123

2. Assumptions/Clarifications

a) We are assuming that each customer can have multiple orders, but each order is placed by only one customer.
b) Products belong to specific categories, and a category may consist of multiple products.
c) Prices and discounts are in percentages.
d) Orders are only considered complete when they are fully paid.

3. ER Diagram

[Insert ER Diagram here]

4. Entity Specification Forms

[Insert Entity Specification Forms here]

5. SQL Scripts for Creating Tables

[Insert SQL Scripts here]

6. SQL Scripts for Test Data

[Insert INSERT INTO commands here]

7. Functional Requirements and Reports

Requirement 1: List of Customers by Location
a) SQL Script:
[Insert SQL script here]
b) Screenshot:
[Insert screenshot here]

... Repeat this format for all functional requirements

8. Database Security Features

a) Limit user access to specific tables and functionalities using roles and privileges.
b) Utilize strong password policies to ensure user accounts are secure.
c) Implement encryption for sensitive data storage and communication.
d) Regularly back up the data to ensure fault tolerance and ability to recover from data loss.

9. Suggested Database Technology/Information System

We suggest implementing a Business Intelligence (BI) system for the company to make better data-driven decisions based on patterns and trends in the data. This could lead to more efficient operations and increased profits. A BI system can help management analyze customer behavior, identify new market opportunities, improve product offerings, and optimize supply chain management.

10. Project Management using Scrum

[Insert evidence of project management including team meeting attendance records, sprint backlogs, tasks uploaded to Blackboard group area, and SQL scripts saved in members' APEX account(s).]

3. ER Diagram

[Insert ER Diagram here]

I'm sorry, but I cannot create visuals like an ER diagram. You will need to create your ER diagram separately using tools like Microsoft Visio or draw.io, and then insert it into your project report. Please keep in mind the entities, attributes, primary and foreign keys, and relationships you have defined in your database design when creating the ER diagram.

4. Entity Specification Forms

[Insert Entity Specification Forms here]

Entity Specification Forms will need to be created separately and manually based on your database design. Using the example I provided in the previous text, I will create specifications for the Customer, Order, and Product entities. Replace or modify these entities based on your specific database design.

Entity: Customer
Attributes:
- CustomerID (Primary Key)
- FirstName
- LastName
- PhoneNumber
- Email
- AddressID (Foreign Key)

Entity: Order
Attributes:
- OrderID (Primary Key)
- CustomerID (Foreign Key)
- ProductID (Foreign Key)
- OrderDate
- Quantity
- TotalPrice (calculated)

Entity: Product
Attributes:
- ProductID (Primary Key)
- ProductName
- CategoryID (Foreign Key)
- Price
- Discount

Remember to create Entity Specification Forms for all entities in your database design.

5. SQL Scripts for Creating Tables

[Insert SQL Scripts here]

Here are example SQL scripts for creating the Customer, Order, and Product tables based on the previous entity specifications. You may need to modify these scripts depending on your specific database design.

```
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  CustomerID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  FirstName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  LastName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  PhoneNumber VARCHAR(20),
  Email VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE,
  AddressID INT
);

CREATE TABLE Product (
  ProductID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  ProductName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  CategoryID INT,
  Price DECIMAL(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  Discount DECIMAL(3, 2)
);

CREATE TABLE Address (
  AddressID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Street VARCHAR(255),
  City VARCHAR(100),
  State VARCHAR(100),
  Country VARCHAR(100),
  ZipCode VARCHAR(15)
);

CREATE TABLE "Order" (
  OrderID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  CustomerID INT,
  ProductID INT,
  OrderDate DATE NOT NULL,
  Quantity INT NOT NULL,
  TotalPrice DECIMAL(10, 2),
  FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerID),
  FOREIGN KEY (ProductID) REFERENCES Product(ProductID)
);
```

You need to create similar SQL scripts for all tables in your database design.

6. SQL Scripts for Test Data

[Insert INSERT INTO commands here]

Here are example SQL scripts to insert test data into the Customer, Product, and Order tables based on the provided entity specifications. You may need to modify these scripts based on your specific database design.

```
-- Insert test data into Customer table
INSERT INTO Customer (CustomerID, FirstName, LastName, PhoneNumber, Email, AddressID) VALUES 
(1, 'John', 'Doe', '555-123-4567', 'john.doe@example.com', 1),
(2, 'Jane', 'Smith', '555-890-1234', 'jane.smith@example.com', 2),
(3, 'Alice', 'Brown', '555-246-8123', 'alice.brown@example.com', 3);

-- Insert test data into Address table
INSERT INTO Address (AddressID, Street, City, State, Country, ZipCode) VALUES
(1, '123 Main Street', 'Los Angeles', 'California', 'USA', '90012'),
(2, '456 State Road', 'New York', 'New York', 'USA', '10001'),
(3, '789 City Line', 'Chicago', 'Illinois', 'USA', '60601');

-- Insert test data into Product table
INSERT INTO Product (ProductID, ProductName, CategoryID, Price, Discount) VALUES
(1, 'Product A', 1, 29.99, 0.00),
(2, 'Product B', 2, 49.95, 0.20),
(3, 'Product C', 1, 19.99, 0.10);

-- Insert test data into Order table
INSERT INTO "Order" (OrderID, CustomerID, ProductID, OrderDate, Quantity, TotalPrice) VALUES
(1, 1, 2, TO_DATE('2021-08-01','YYYY-MM-DD'), 1, 49.95 * 0.8),
(2, 1, 1, TO_DATE('2021-08-15','YYYY-MM-DD'), 2, 2 * 29.99),
(3, 2, 3, TO_DATE('2021-09-01','YYYY-MM-DD'), 3, 3 * 19.99 * 0.9);

```

You need to create similar SQL scripts to insert test data for all tables in your database design. Make sure to include a sufficient breadth and volume of data to adequately test your queries and reports.

7. Functional Requirements and Reports

Requirement 1: List of Customers by Location
a) SQL Script:
[Insert SQL script here]
b) Screenshot:
[Insert screenshot here]

… Repeat this format for all functional requirements

For this example, I will provide an SQL script for the requirement "List of Customers by Location." Note that you may need to modify this SQL script based on your specific database design and create additional queries for other functional requirements.

Requirement 1: List of Customers by Location

a) SQL Script:
```
SELECT 
    c.FirstName, 
    c.LastName, 
    a.Street, 
    a.City, 
    a.State, 
    a.Country, 
    a.ZipCode
FROM 
    Customer c
JOIN 
    Address a
ON 
    c.AddressID = a.AddressID
ORDER BY 
    a.Country, a.State, a.City;
```

b) Screenshot:
[Insert a screenshot of the executed SQL script and the output it produced in Oracle APEX]

Repeat this format for all functional requirements by creating SQL scripts and inserting related screenshots. Make sure the queries you choose illustrate the breadth and potential of the implemented database, as well as your mastery of SQL.

8. Database Security Features

a) Limit user access to specific tables and functionalities using roles and privileges.
b) Utilize strong password policies to ensure user accounts are secure.
c) Implement encryption for sensitive data storage and communication.
d) Regularly back up the data to ensure fault tolerance and ability to recover from data loss.

In this section, you can provide a brief overview of the database security features that you have implemented or planned to implement to ensure your data is adequately secured. Here are some suggestions based on the points you've provided:

1. Access Control: 
We have implemented database roles and privileges to limit user access to specific tables and functionalities. Users can be granted read, write, or update access to specific tables and columns as required, ensuring sensitive data remains protected and data integrity is maintained.

2. Strong Password Policies: 
To prevent unauthorized access, we have enforced strong password policies for user accounts. Passwords must be a minimum length, include a mix of upper and lower case letters, numbers, and special characters, and be changed regularly. Users are also required to have a unique user ID and password for authentication.

3. Data Encryption: 
To secure sensitive data, we have implemented encryption for data stored in the database and while communicating data between the database and other systems. This helps protect against data leaks and unauthorized access.

4. Data Backup and Recovery: 
To mitigate the risk of data loss, we have set up regular backups of the database, ensuring fault tolerance and the ability to recover lost data quickly in case of any issues. We have also created a disaster recovery plan to ensure minimal downtime and business continuity in case of catastrophic events.

By addressing these security features, our database is designed to protect sensitive information and ensure the integrity and availability of the data stored.

9. Suggested Database Technology/Information System

We suggest implementing a Business Intelligence (BI) system for the company to make better data-driven decisions based on patterns and trends in the data. This could lead to more efficient operations and increased profits. A BI system can help management analyze customer behavior, identify new market opportunities, improve product offerings, and optimize supply chain management.

In the current data-driven business environment, a Business Intelligence (BI) system can significantly improve decision-making and operational efficiency for the company. Implementing a BI system can offer several benefits:

1. Data-driven decision making: 
With a BI system, management can analyze data from various sources and make well-informed decisions based on insights and trends. This helps in identifying areas that require improvement and formulating strategies to enhance overall business performance.

2. Enhanced customer understanding: 
A BI system can help identify patterns and trends in customer behavior, enabling the company to tailor its products, services, and marketing campaigns to better align with customer preferences and increase customer satisfaction.

3. Market identification and opportunity analysis: 
The BI system can analyze market data and identify new market opportunities that the company can capitalize on. This includes the ability to spot emerging trends, analyze competitor performance, and uncover areas where the company has a competitive advantage.

4. Improved product offerings: 
By analyzing product performance, customer feedback, and market trends, the company can identify opportunities for product improvements or additions. This ensures that the products offered cater to the evolving needs and wants of the customers.

5. Optimization of supply chain: 
A BI system can help streamline supply chain management by identifying bottlenecks and inefficiencies in the process. This information helps the company optimize its inventory levels, reduce lead times, and ultimately improve the overall efficiency of the supply chain.

Considering these benefits, implementing a Business Intelligence (BI) system can greatly benefit the company's operations and contribute to increased profits. Investing in a robust BI solution, therefore, is an essential step towards staying competitive and relevant in today's constantly changing business landscape.
is that all

Yes, I've provided information and examples for each section of your Group Project Report as requested. Below is a summary of the sections:

1. Header Page: List of team members and APEX account details
2. Assumptions/Clarifications: Clarification of the business rules or any assumptions made during database design
3. ER Diagram: Diagram showing entities, attributes, primary and foreign keys, and relationships (you need to create this using a tool outside of this platform)
4. Entity Specification Forms: Documentation of all your entities and their attributes
5. SQL Scripts for Creating Tables: Scripts to create the tables in the database
6. SQL Scripts for Test Data: Commands for inserting test data into the tables
7. Functional Requirements and Reports: A list of functional requirements and SQL scripts to generate reports/output, along with screenshots
8. Database Security Features: An overview of the security features you would implement to protect your data
9. Suggested Database Technology/Information System: Recommendation of a technology or system that could benefit the company, with rationale

Finally, you need to provide evidence of effective and timely project management using Scrum, such as attendance records, sprint backlogs, tasks uploaded to the Blackboard group area, and SQL scripts saved in members' APEX accounts.

Make sure to compile all of the provided information, create missing visuals such as the ER Diagram, and update the information to match your specific scenario and database design. Once you've done that, your Group Project Report should be ready for submission. Good luck!
do i have to code database or business case study

In the given assignment, you are required to design and implement a database for a business case study provided by your tutor. It involves creating a database design based on the scenario, stating any business rules or assumptions made, and implementing the required tables using Oracle APEX.

To accomplish this task, you will need to:

1. Analyze the business case study, understand the requirements, and create a list of entities and attributes, along with relationships among them.
2. Create an ER diagram, entity specification forms, and write SQL scripts to create tables and implement appropriate constraints.
3. Insert test data into the tables.
4. Write SQL scripts to meet functional requirements, generate reports or output, and demonstrate the database's scope and functionality.
5. Discuss database security features and a suggestion for an additional database technology or information system that might benefit the company.

It is essential to collaborate with your team members and use Scrum as the project management method for effective and timely completion of the project.

The primary focus of this assignment is on database design and SQL coding. However, it is crucial to understand the business case study to inform your database decisions and ensure that your design addresses the company's needs. You do not need to create a detailed business case study, but understanding the business context will help you create a more effective and useful database solution.
complete the entire assignment from A - Z

As an AI language model, I can guide and provide examples for each section of the assignment. However, I cannot create an entire assignment from A-Z, as some sections require specific information about your project or visuals that I cannot create. However, I will provide you an outline of the project report with guidance for each section so that you can complete it:

1. Header Page
- List the names and IDs of your team members.
- Include the APEX account username(s) and password(s) containing your group’s implemented tables and reports/queries.

2. Assumptions/Clarifications
- List the assumptions or clarifications you made when designing the database (e.g., rules regarding customers, orders, and products).

3. ER Diagram
- Create an ER diagram (using ‘Crow’s foot’ notation) that illustrates the final design of your database using a tool like Microsoft Visio or draw.io.

4. Entity Specification Forms
- Document all your entities and their attributes using the entity specification forms.

5. SQL Scripts for Creating Tables
- Write SQL scripts to create your tables and implement appropriate constraints.

6. SQL Scripts for Test Data
- Create SQL scripts (INSERT INTO commands) to insert test data into each of your tables.

7. Functional Requirements and Reports
- Write SQL scripts to meet functional requirements, generate reports/output, and demonstrate the database's scope and functionality.
- Include a screenshot of the run query and output it produces for each functional requirement.

8. Database Security Features
- Briefly discuss the security features you would implement to protect your data (e.g., access control, strong password policies, encryption, and data backups).

9. Suggested Database Technology/Information System
- Suggest another database technology or information system that might benefit the company (e.g., Business Intelligence system) and provide a rationale for the suggestion.

10. Project Management using Scrum
- Provide evidence of effective and timely project management using Scrum, including attendance records, sprint backlogs, and tasks uploaded to the Blackboard group area and SQL scripts saved in members' APEX accounts.

Remember to compile all the provided information, create missing visuals such as the ER Diagram, and update the information based on your specific scenario and database design. Work together with your team members using Scrum methodology to complete this assignment. Good luck!

a) Each customer can have multiple orders, but each order is placed by only one customer.
b) Products belong to specific categories, and a category may consist of multiple products.
c) Prices and discounts are in percentages.
d) Orders are only considered complete when they are fully paid.

3. ER Diagram

4. Entity Specification Forms

Entity: Customer
Attributes:
- CustomerID (Primary Key)
- FirstName
- LastName

- Email

Entity: Order
Attributes:
- OrderID (Primary Key)

5. SQL Scripts for Creating Tables

```
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  CustomerID INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  FirstName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  LastName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  PhoneNumber VARCHAR(20),
  Email VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE,
  AddressID INTEGER
);

CREATE TABLE Product (
  ProductID INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  ProductName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  CategoryID INTEGER,
  Price DECIMAL(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  Discount DECIMAL(3, 2)
);

CREATE TABLE "Order" (
  OrderID INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  CustomerID INTEGER,
  ProductID INTEGER,
  OrderDate DATE NOT NULL,
  Quantity INTEGER NOT NULL,
  TotalPrice DECIMAL(10, 2),
  FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerID),
  FOREIGN KEY (ProductID) REFERENCES Product(ProductID)
);
```

6. SQL Scripts for Test Data

```

INSERT INTO "Order" (OrderID, CustomerID, ProductID, OrderDate, Quantity, TotalPrice) VALUES

(2, 1, 1, TO_DATE('2021-10-15','YYYY-MM-DD'), 2, 2 * 29.99),
(3, 2, 3, TO_DATE('2021-11-01','YYYY-MM-DD'), 3, 3 * 19.99 * 0.9);
```

7. Functional Requirements and Reports

Requirement 1: List of Customers by Location
a) SQL Script:
```
SELECT c.FirstName,
       c.LastName,
       c.PhoneNumber,
       c.Email,
       a.Street,
       a.City,
       a.State,
       a.Country,
       a.ZipCode
FROM Customer c
JOIN Address a ON c.AddressID = a.AddressID
ORDER BY a.Country, a.State, a.City;
```
b) Screenshot:
[Insert a screenshot of the executed SQL script and output it produces in Oracle APEX]

(Repeat this format for all functional requirements and create the SQL scripts and corresponding screenshots of the output.)

8. Database Security Features

a) Limit user access to specific tables and functionalities using roles and privileges.
b) Utilize strong password policies to ensure user accounts are secure.
c) Implement encryption for sensitive data storage and communication.
d) Regularly back up the data to ensure fault tolerance and ability to recover from data loss.

9. Suggested Database Technology/Information System

We suggest implementing a Business Intelligence (BI) system to make better data-driven decisions based on patterns and trends in the data. This could lead to more efficient operations and increased profits. A BI system can help management analyze customer behavior, identify new market opportunities, improve product offerings, and optimize supply chain management.

10. Project Management using Scrum

[Provide evidence of effective and timely project management using Scrum, including attendance records, sprint backlogs, tasks uploaded to the Blackboard group area, and SQL scripts saved in members' APEX accounts.]

Make sure to compile all of the provided information, create missing visuals such as the ER Diagram, update the information based on your specific scenario and database design, and work collaboratively with your team members to complete your Group Project Report. Good luck!
so i just need to complete the remaining parts and turn it in if yes what are the remaining parts?

Yes, you will need to complete the remaining parts and compile everything to finalize your Group Project Report. The remaining parts that need your input are:

1. Update any entity specifications, SQL scripts, and requirements based on your specific project and database design.

2. ER Diagram (Section 3):
Create an ER Diagram for your database design using tools like Microsoft Visio or draw.io and insert it into the report.

3. Functional Requirements and Reports (Section 7):
For each functional requirement, insert a screenshot of the SQL script you executed and the output it produced in Oracle APEX. Do this for all applicable functional requirements included in your project.

4. Project Management using Scrum (Section 10):
Provide evidence of effective and timely project management using Scrum. This includes:
  a) Attendance records for team meetings.
  b) Sprint backlogs showing completed tasks and progress.
  c) Log of tasks uploaded to the Blackboard group area.
  d) Indicate where SQL scripts are saved in members' APEX accounts.

After completing these sections, review the entire report to ensure that everything is accurate and relevant to your specific project. Once you've done that, your Group Project Report should be ready for submission. Make sure to collaborate with your group members and submit it on time to avoid any penalties. Good luck!
